pck.tips.title: Quick Tips for Stronger Passwords
pck.tip1.title: Use 12+ Characters
pck.tip1.desc: Longer passwords exponentially increase crack time. Each character adds significant complexity.
pck.tip2.title: Mix Character Types
pck.tip2.desc: Combine uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols to maximize entropy.
pck.tip3.title: Avoid Common Patterns
pck.tip3.desc: Skip dictionary words, keyboard patterns, and personal info like birthdays.
pck.tip4.title: Use a Password Manager
pck.tip4.desc: Generate and store unique, complex passwords for each account securely.
pck.tip5.title: Generate Strong Passwords
pck.tip5.desc: Use our built-in generator or a password manager to create 20+ character random passwords that are virtually impossible to crack.
pck.about.title: About This Tool
pck.about.desc: Our password strength checker uses the zxcvbn library to analyze your password in real-time. It detects common patterns, dictionary words, and estimates how long it would take to crack.

pck.privacy.label: Privacy First
pck.privacy.title: Your Password Never Leaves Your Browser
pck.privacy.body1: We take your privacy extremely seriously. Unlike some online password checkers that send your password to their servers for analysis, our tool performs all calculations directly in your browser using JavaScript. This means your password stays on your device and is never transmitted over the internet.
pck.privacy.body2: You can verify this yourself by checking your browser's network inspector - you won't see any password data being sent anywhere. The entire analysis uses the open-source zxcvbn library, which runs entirely client-side.
pck.about.label: About Password Security
pck.about.seo.title: Understanding Password Strength and Security
pck.about.body1: Password security is one of the most critical aspects of online safety, yet it remains one of the most misunderstood. A strong password is your first line of defense against unauthorized access to your accounts, personal information, and digital assets. Every year, millions of accounts are compromised due to weak or reused passwords, leading to identity theft, financial loss, and reputational damage. Understanding how password strength is measured and how attackers try to compromise passwords can help you create better, more secure passwords that truly protect your digital life. Modern password cracking techniques have become increasingly sophisticated, using powerful GPUs, rainbow tables, and machine learning algorithms to guess passwords at rates of billions of attempts per second. What seemed like a secure password a few years ago may now be vulnerable to these advanced attacks. Attackers also use credential stuffing, where they take passwords leaked from one data breach and try them on other services, exploiting the common habit of password reuse across multiple websites and applications.
pck.about.body2: Password strength is typically measured using entropy, which represents the amount of uncertainty or randomness in a password. The higher the entropy, the more difficult a password is to crack through brute force methods. Entropy is measured in bits, and each bit doubles the number of possible combinations a password cracker must try. For example, a password with 40 bits of entropy has over one trillion possible combinations, while a password with 80 bits of entropy would take even the most powerful supercomputers billions of years to exhaust. However, entropy alone doesn't tell the whole story. A password like 'P@ssw0rd123!' might have decent entropy on paper, but it's actually very weak because it follows common patterns that attackers know to check first. Hackers use dictionaries of known substitutions, common keyboard walks like 'qwerty' or 'asdf', leaked password databases, and contextual information to dramatically reduce the effective search space. This is why our checker uses sophisticated pattern recognition in addition to entropy calculations, providing you with a much more realistic assessment of your password's real-world security.
pck.about.body3: The key to creating truly strong passwords is to combine length with genuine randomness. A passphrase made of four or more random words (like 'correct-horse-battery-staple') can be both strong and memorable, offering far more entropy than a shorter password with mixed character types. Alternatively, use a reputable password manager to generate and store complex, unique passwords for each of your accounts. Enable two-factor authentication whenever possible, as it provides an essential additional layer of security even if your password is somehow compromised. Remember that the weakest link in your security chain is often reused passwords: if one service is breached, all your other accounts using the same password become immediately vulnerable. Our password strength checker helps you understand the real-world strength of your passwords by estimating how long it would take to crack them using modern techniques, identifying specific weaknesses, and providing actionable improvement suggestions. Take a moment to test your commonly used passwords and see where you can improve your overall security posture.
pck.faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
pck.faq.q1: Is it safe to enter my real password here?
pck.faq.a1: Yes, completely safe. Your password never leaves your browser - all analysis happens locally using JavaScript. We don't store, log, or transmit your password to any server. You can verify this by checking your browser's network inspector.
pck.faq.a2: We use the zxcvbn library, which analyzes password patterns, dictionary words, and character combinations to estimate crack time. Unlike simple entropy calculators, it recognizes common patterns that make passwords weak despite appearing complex.
pck.faq.a3: A very strong password typically has 12+ characters, uses a mix of character types, avoids dictionary words and patterns, and would take centuries or longer to crack. Random passwords generated by password managers usually achieve this rating.
pck.faq.q4: Why is my 'complex' password still showing as weak?
pck.faq.a4: Complex-looking passwords like 'P@ssw0rd!' are weak because they follow predictable patterns that attackers check first. Substituting numbers for letters or adding symbols at the end doesn't fool modern cracking tools. True strength comes from randomness, not just complexity.
pck.faq.q5: Should I use the same password if it rates as 'Very Strong'?
pck.faq.a5: No, never reuse passwords across different accounts. Even the strongest password becomes a liability if one service is breached. Use a password manager to generate and store unique, strong passwords for each account you have.
